summary: Change citation format from the right-click context menu.

description.text:
### Checklist

- [x] Zettlr does not possess this feature (if it does, but the feature doesn't work out for you, propose an enhancement instead.)
- [ ] Zettlr's current features cannot be used to approximate this behaviour (otherwise, propose an enhancement instead.)
- [ ] I have searched the documentation to make sure I did not overlook it.
- [ ] I have searched both open and closed issues to see if someone has already proposed this.

### Feature Proposal

When editing a document, I often decide to change between the following in-editor citation formats:

- `[@barnouwTowerBabelHistory1966, 224]` → (Barnouw 1966, 224)
- `barnouwTowerBabelHistory1966` → Barnouw (1966)
- `@barnouwTowerBabelHistory1966 [224]` → Barnouw (1966, 224)

For example, I might change a sentence reading:

`Barnouw (1966, 224) notes that Paramount bought into CBS with a 49% stake in September 1928.`

…to:

`Paramount bought into CBS with a 49% stake in September 1928 (Barnouw 1966, 224).`

It's simple enough to move the `@barnouwTowerBabelHistory1966 [224]` citation from the beginning of the sentence to the end (or vice versa), either by dragging and dropping it or using cut-and-paste, but afterward I also have to edit it, changing `@barnouwTowerBabelHistory1966 [224]` to `[@barnouwTowerBabelHistory1966, 224]` or retyping the whole citation from scratch. Editing the citation, moreover, is always a little wonky because autocomplete usually tries to re-render it at least once as I'm making the changes, momentarily hiding my cursor.

I think it'd be nice to streamline the process of converting between different forms of the citekey shortcode by adding this option to the right-click context menu for the citation. Currently, when I right-click a rendered citation, I get the following context menu:

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/df6e0175-6323-4c42-9685-e3fa8efdc641)

I'm proposing to add these options to the context menu:
- `Convert to parenthetical citation` (to convert `@barnouwTowerBabelHistory1966 [224]` to `[@barnouwTowerBabelHistory1966, 224]`)
- `Convert to narrative citation` (to convert `[@barnouwTowerBabelHistory1966, 224]` to `@barnouwTowerBabelHistory1966 [224]`).

An additional option might be `Remove page numbers` to omit the page locations from the in-text citation, thereby converting `@barnouwTowerBabelHistory1966 [224]` to `@barnouwTowerBabelHistory1966` or `[@barnouwTowerBabelHistory1966, 224]` to `[@barnouwTowerBabelHistory1966]`.


### Example Workflow

See above.

### Caveats

None.

### Do you wish to attempt implementing this yourself?

No

### Platforms

- [ ] Windows
- [ ] macOS
- [X] Linux

### Additional Information

_No response_

### Zettlr version

summary: Extremely slow performance on Mac (same issue as #5019)

description.text:
### Description

Extremely slow performance in the Zettlr app, when other applications on this Mac are performing acceptably. The performance is excruciating. When I open a new file via the + tool, it can take 15 or 30 seconds to see the new window, but, after I see the new window, another 30 seconds or so before an insert cursor appears. Then when I can insert, the insert cannot keep up with my typing, and falls further and further behind.

Amazingly, I still find Zettlr worth the annoyance.

I am running Zettlr 3.4.1, UUID: 7f1e155e-93ca-4c36-b780-197d80a0c859.

MacBook Pro 2.3 Ghz Dual-Core Intel Core i5
Ventura 13.7.2
16 GB 2133 MHz LPDDR3

I now have 3294 items in the workspace, almost all small text items, but a few dozen images of sizes up to 1.2 MB. However, until yesterday, I did have about twenty large files in the same directory as my Zettlr workspace. These were PDFs and CSVs of 100-350 MB. They were related to my project, but, not files that were useful through Zettlr. Just yesterday it occurred to me that perhaps Zettlr was having to process these large files, and by moving them to a different directory, I might make Zettlr more responsive. Perhaps it helped, but there has been no significant improvement.

I have cleared the FSAL cache multiple times, again with no significant improvement.

### Reproducing

This occurs every time I use Zettlr

### Zettlr version

3.4.1, UUID: 7f1e155e-93ca-4c36-b780-197d80a0c859

### Installation Method

From the Website or GitHub

### Installation Method (Other)

_No response_

### Operating System Version

Ventura 13.7.2

### Additional Information

MacBook Pro 2.3 Ghz Dual-Core Intel Core i5
Ventura 13.7.2
16 GB 2133 MHz LPDDR3

### Description

It would be nice to be able to toggle spell check on and off without having to dig around the preferences.

### Proposed Changes

Currently to activate or deactivate spell check, you have to go into preferences -> spell check -> and check/uncheck dictionaries. Then you have to quit the app and reopen it for the changes to take effect.

Ideally we would want the changes to take effect immediately. Also it would be really nice if there was a button and keyboard command to activate or deactivate the spell checking feature as well.

Another mode that would be nice to have is only spellchecking lines the user has touched (similar to Typora) rather than displaying red underlines all over the document.

### Caveats

None.

### Do you Wish to Attempt Implementing this Yourself?

No

### Zettlr Version

Stable (most recent version)

### Your Platform

- [X] Windows
- [X] macOS
- [X] Linux

### Operating System Version

_No response_

### Additional Information

_No response_

summary: Add padding around text in code blocks

description.text:
### Description

Padding around code blocks would improve the aesthetics of the editor.

### Proposed Changes

The text hugs the edge of the gray background right now. Ideally there would be some padding moving the text away from the edges of the box on all 4 sides.

Even better if this could be configured via CSS or the GUI.

### Caveats

No caveats.

### Do you Wish to Attempt Implementing this Yourself?

Yes

### Zettlr Version

Stable (most recent version)

### Your Platform

- [X] Windows
- [X] macOS
- [X] Linux

### Operating System Version

_No response_

### Additional Information

I tried to implement this using custom CSS, but every implementation I came up with was super hacky and didn't quite work because of how the background color is implemented here: https://github.com/Zettlr/Zettlr/blob/088e35e63529dba2d623ba27aa6d53144eda180d/source/common/modules/markdown-editor/plugins/code-background.ts

Not sure exactly what with the codemirror/view / layer API would need to be edited to add this padding, but I'm guessing the fix would be somewhere in that file.

summary: Improve auto-suggestions for recently used citations via the "accessed" field in the references JSON file.

description.text:
### Description

I love that when I type the `@` symbol to begin adding a reference, Zettlr now suggests citations already used in the document. This is incredibly helpful and a great time saver. I was thinking the feature might be further improved by sorting subsequent suggestions by the date given in the "accessed" field of the JSON document from which Zettlr draws the references.

Since Zotero and other citation managers typically auto-populate the "accessed" field with the date a citation was added, this would allow Zettlr to prioritize the most recently added citations first in the auto-suggested list (the user could, of course, still filter the list with their subsequent typing).

I think this would be helpful, as I often find myself adding new references to Zotero specifically so that I can cite them in the Zettlr document I'm currently working on. Given this workflow, it's obvious why it would be handy for Zettlr to bring the newly added reference to the top of the auto-suggested list.

### Proposed Changes

See above. I don't propose doing away with the extremely useful auto-suggestions for citations already included in the document. Rather, I'm proposing that you keep these and simply add the suggestions based on the `accessed` field immediately below in the list.

### Caveats

The only snag I can think of is that there may be a resource cost in sorting data from the whole JSON document by reverse-chronological order of the `accessed` field. If a person's reference list extends to thousands of citations, the JSON file may be several megabytes in size. That said, I don't think the cost would be that significant, and the sorting algorithm would only need to run when the JSON file is modified, which is a relatively infrequent occurrence.

### Do you Wish to Attempt Implementing this Yourself?

No

### Zettlr Version

Nightly

### Your Platform

- [ ] Windows
- [ ] macOS
- [X] Linux

### Operating System Version

Manjaro (Rolling)

### Additional Information

_No response_
